speaker: interviewer
text: Interview on June 9th, 1995, with Norman Salsitz, taking place in Springfield, New Jersey, United States of America. I'm Rhoda [INAUDIBLE], and the interview is being conducted in English. Would you continue, please, with the pogrom? You said that it was-- it was held shortly after the anniversary of the independence of Poland, with the celebrating the constitution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they were so happy that they even composed-- they took a Polish song, which was a cheery song, and it's a beautiful song. [SINGING IN POLISH]. It means, 'We greet you. The-- the-- the-- the stars never think of May.' And the ref-- the end was-- they changed it-- [SINGING IN POLISH]. And it means, 'The 3rd of May and the 6th of May.' Because the 3rd of May was the national holiday, and the 6th of May was the pogrom. The 3rd of May and the 6th of May, break all the windows by the Jews. [SINGING IN POLISH]. 'Beat the Jew-- kill the Jew. Because for the Poles is a wonderful, heavenly time.' In this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- these are the words in those beautiful lyrics you sang?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was the words with the lyrics that they changed. They changed from the official lyrics. The official lyrics were beautiful, because it was an ode the May-- to the month of-- month of May-- and to the Constitution of the 3rd of May. So they changed it to the pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So till before the war, and we had the quarrel with the Pole, so we always to tell them, what do you think, this is the 6th of May? So we used to tell them this. Aha. Now, this pogrom took place a year before your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly to the date. A year--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, during the years in which you were in the town, growing up, in the cheder and then in the public school, do you remember any particular incidents of antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially in the schools. I want to tell you two episodes. And I will make them short, because we don't have time--

speaker: interviewer
text: In cheder, or in the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school. The cheder, I didn't have no antisemitism in cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: No problem going back and forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: No problem [INAUDIBLE]. Only in school. So in school, there was-- I was in this time-- I think I was nine years old-- no, I was seven years old. So we got a new teacher. The new teacher's name was Wadislaw Wishnewsky. And he was a reserve officer of the Polish army, and he became a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he probably never encountered Jewish boys, with peyos, with yarmulkes, and he didn't like it. So he got together four classes of the Jewish boys, and he put a teacher-- a Polish teacher-- we used to call her the [NON-ENGLISH] because she was very tall-- 'the long one'-- not to let out the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, he came. He had in one hand-- one hand was a white glove, the other hand he had a clipper. And he went from-- he went from boy to boy, and he cut off one earlock, because all the children have earlocks. He cut off one earlock, from the earlock to the middle of the head. Because he understood, if they will come home with one earlock, the parents will cut off the second earlock. Because, after all-- and he started it, one after [INAUDIBLE]. And he had four classes, and the four classes probably had about 60 or 80 Jewish children. All the Jewish children started to cry. And they were seven, eight years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, in this time, seven or eight. And when he came closer to me, and I saw all my friends are starting to cry, but behind me was sitting Moishele. And Moishele was the son of the [? dayan, ?] of the assistant rabbi. And Moishele had the longest peyos. He had peyos that they reached him to the waist. And I couldn't imagine that Moishele would go home with one [? peye. ?] I even didn't do it maybe so much for me as for Moishele. And I was always a reb-- a rebel. I always stood up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even at seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even at seven. I always did-- so I jumped on the-- on the bench. We had benches. And from one bench, I jumped to other, and to other. And when I came to the last bench, I-- the teacher was standing by the door, not to let out nobody. So I, with my feet, jumped into her. And when I jumped, she fell down. And I ran out, and I started to yell, all the way [NON-ENGLISH]. It means 'Daddy, they're cutting the peyos.' Till I came to our store, which was not too far. And I came to our store, and I told it to my father. And when my father heard that the teacher is cutting the peyos, which was unheard. So he went to a few other neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it happened one neighbor was a director of a bank-- a Jewish bank. And all of them we were running to the school. And I remember my father was running. He had a long kapota, and the kapota was not buttoned, and it was flowing. And they came into the school, and they went to the principal. And so happened that the principal was a very good friend of my father, because the principal came from the same village as my father. They were friends. And he said, what's going on? The principal said he didn't know anything. And then he went into the school, and they see 60 children, sitting, crying, everybody with one peye. Right away-- Including Moishe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Moishe was behind me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he ran with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't run. But I was a bench before him. And when I jumped out, he stopped cutting. He knew that-- he stopped cutting. And Moishele, till today-- this Moishele-- Rubin is his name-- he's a very famous rabbi in Monsey, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: He survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived in Russia. He's a very famous rabbi in Monsey. He has his own school. And every time we meet, he tells everybody, you know, I have to tell you a story like-- when Naftula saved my peyos. Till today he tells them this story. Naturally, he stopped it. And the Jewish-- my father went to Lemberg. And Lemberg was the curatorium. It was the school-- the offices. And the teacher was suspended, and he started-- and he started a suit to sue the teacher for what he did. The next day-- right away, they brought a photographer, and the next day, the whole in Poland-- Jewish papers, in all the Jewish papers, were children with one peye. And they wrote, what happened in Kolbuszowa is that the teacher came to cut the peyos. It was in Jewish paper in Warsaw, in Lemberg, in Lwow, and Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, anyway, the case came to court. When the case came to court, a few non-Jewish, important people came to other Jews, and they came to my father. And they said, why do you have to rock the boat? It's better-- he promised not to do it anymore. Let's withdraw the cha-- the complaint. And Jews in [INAUDIBLE] in the diaspora, maybe. So my father figured, why should we have enemies, if only he was very involved. And he went, and he withdrew the charges, and the teacher became teacher again. All right. Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is-- when I was-- now-- but I have to tell you another story--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, now this is when you were seven years old. And there are other stories of other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one more story I will tell you, and then I wouldn't-- the other story, when I was nine years, with the same teacher. And this teacher was the music teacher by us. Other subjects, also. And he prepared a show. The second important holiday-- national holiday-- was the 19th of March, because the 19th of March is Saint Joseph's Day. And the marshal of Poland was Jozef Pilsudski, and he was very much admired by Jews and by non-Jews. So the Saint Joseph's play-- and what is his name's day. But Jews, we-- here in America, we don't have name days. We have birthdays. By us, we had name days. So for this holiday, he prepared a show. But there was a beautiful song about Pilsudski. And he couldn't find a boy in the class to sing this song.

speaker: interviewer
text: So happens, I was nine years old. And in this time I already sang with a very famous cantor. The cantor was from Berlin, but he was from our town. He used to come to us-- give us a treat. So he made a choir-- organized-- and he picked me out to the choir.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a young boy, I had a boy soprano, which is a very unusual voice, for one. So he tried out who will sing this song. And from all the boys, non-Jews, he was very, very depressed with it. Said he had to pick me out. I should sing this song. And now he taught me with the violin. I came home. My father said, you are crazy. This is not your holiday. Your holiday is in the temple. [INAUDIBLE] But I was so happy. I said, I will be able to sing to my sisters. He taught me. And we prepared the show. All dignitaries came to the show all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father permitted you to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, kindly-- he perm-- I was the youngest. I was the ninth. My mother told him-- and the sisters-- I said, he wants it. So he permitted it. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- the day of the show-- this was the 19th of March, 1929. The day of the March, I came-- I went in the morning to shul, to pray a little more to God he should look favorable, I should sing good, and all those things. And I came home. My mother prepared me the jacket and prepared me the pants. And she took my yarmulke-- you know, the kippah? It was from velvet. And she put her hand in kerosene, and she cleaned it with kerosene, to have a luster. When you clean velvet with kerosene-- I remember those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to school, and I was so happy. When they started the show, people, dignitaries, they were coming in. And then it was my time to sing. So they took me on the stage. And from the stage, they took my hand, and they had prepared a divider in the corner. And they put me behind the divider. And he said, you will sing from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I started to choke. Tears started to show. I mean, what do you mean I sing here? You said I have the best voice. And now the people will not see me. My parents will not see me. And right away, before I start to think, I could-- he, with the violin, started to play. And automatically, automatically, I started to sing. [SINGING IN POLISH]. You still remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN POLISH] It means, our Pilsudski, our master. Grandpa, like we called him, because we called him [NON-ENGLISH]. He was so adored by us. And with his strong fist, he gave us back our dear Poland. And so on, so on. And I sing this song, and I cry. Singing and crying, because I was behind the-- the divider. Nobody saw me. After the show, everybody went out on the stage, bowed, and they applaud. I was sitting there and crying. This was the second time, and I'll never forget it. I was nine years old, and I said, they are ashamed to show my face because I am a Jew. And he picked me out because I was the best one to sing the song. This-- those are things that I never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course. Of course. Now, your life went along. And when did it change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life went along. And then, when I came back from Tarnow, from this yeshiva, I started-- because I didn't want to stay there-- I-- I wanted to study. My dreams were to be-- I wanted always to be a doctor-- a medic-- a medical doctor. Or I wanted to be an actor. One of the two. And then, if I couldn't make a doctor, I want to be an actor, because I used to sing and-- well, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a beautiful singing voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this time it was different. It changed when I was not 13 years old. But I still sing in our temple. And when I-- when I came back, I started to work in my father's store. And I took over-- my father, a Jew with a long beard-- I took over because, in this time, my brother left for Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took his place, and I was the one that I used to go to different towns, bringing in merchandise and delivering the merchandise and packing them. So I worked in our store till-- Did you have a beard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never had a beard. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: But peyos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peyos started to get shorter and shorter. When I was 17 years, the peyos disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you wear a yarmulke all the time-- a kippah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, later, we wear a cap. But till the war, I wore-- I always had covered my head. And-- and-- and a beard-- I started to shave before I had the beard. I remember one day I didn't have a razor, so I-- we sold in our store blades. I took a blade, and I wanted to shave. And I came home, and all my face was cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I never had a beard, and I never-- and the peyos got shorter. And later, when I was 18 years, I threw off my Hasidic clothes and I joined the Hanoar Hatzioni. Do you remember when that was-- what year? You said you were 18, so that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was-- 19, 18-- after, when I-- 17, 18. And I-- in 1937, '38. And I joined the Hanoar Hatzioni. Hanoar Hatzioni is the youth group of the Zionist organization. This was a scout group. And also, I wanted to join there very much, because I dreamt that my girlfriend-- which I loved since I was 12 years, and the first time I spoke to her when I was 18 years. So this was my-- and then she joined, I joined, and eventually I became the head of this Hanoar Hatzioni. And I was the leader of our group. And this happened-- this went on till the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she your motivation for joining?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- I would say-- she was-- I wanted to join anyway, because I wanted to throw off this Hasidic garb. But this was very, very much-- this influenced me very much. No, I joined first. Then later, through me, she joined.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now, this was 1937, 1938, and here you are in Poland. What did you know, if anything, about what was happening in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew very much, because it was close to the Austrian border, and we had German papers. And also, the Polish press imitated. And then in Poland, a lot of things happened that they were very much antisemitic. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew about the Anschluss in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. We lived with it. And Czechoslovakia also. Because we were very much interested in-- my father, even he was a Hasidic Jew, but he was very modern in his ways. Like, for instance, he read a newspaper every day, where other ultra-Orthodox wouldn't read a newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, now, did you-- did you ever think-- at that time, you already had a family member, did you not, one in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one in Israel-- and one in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one in Palestine. Did you-- did any of the others of you think of going-- of leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I came back from the yeshiva, my dream was to go to Palestine. And this was only-- as a matter of fact, in 1939, we couldn't go, because you needed a certificate from the English government to go in. So there was a group, and we called it Aliyah Bet. It was the illegal aliyah. And I app-- made an application with a friend, and we had to pay in $500 and to wait till a illegal ship will leave Constanta, Romania, to go to Palestine. So I sent in, with my friend, and we waited, and we waited and waited. About three months before the war started, finally we got a letter what we should be ready, we should be packed, and we-- because any day we will get a notice to go. And the war started. But I was ready to go. Also, I begged my brother in America. I had a very large family in America. Because my father's six, seven-- seven sisters and brothers-- every one of them was in America with their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except my father, because he was the only one very religious. And he always said that he wants his children should be brought up very Orthodox. And all his brothers, all his sisters, were in America. And then, when I was 18, 19 years, I started to write letters and begged them to send me an affidavit to come to America. And nobody sent me an affidavit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because only-- it would cost them-- later-- it would cost them only $0.25, an affidavit. For $0.25 is because, when they made out an affidavit to bring me over, they had to notarize it. And in this time, if they didn't do business with the bank, they would go to a notary public. And for $0.25, he will notarize a document. And they never--

speaker: interviewer
text: But that can't be the only reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only-- well, not the only reason. I-- this is a-- I'm joking about it. But when I came over and I asked questions, and everybody put it on somebody else. And nobody of them didn't send papers for me, for my sisters, for my brothers. Because in the last few years, everybody wanted to leave Poland. And they needed affidavit. And we had an awful-- we had a large family. We just had-- two years ago, I make a reunion of our family-- a 100-year reunion. Because the first uncles, they came in the 1880s. So I got a reunion. I got 125 people to the reunion in the Catskills. People that they never knew that they had family. It took me a year to find out where to notify the people. And they came to the Catskills. Now we're going to make a second reunion. I will probably have 250 people. And nobody sent me an affidavit. And I didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't hold it against them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I do, but what can I do? I spoke to them when I came over. Everybody-- and everybody told me one thing. We didn't do anything, because you had here a brother. We left it to your brother. And my brother left it to them. And one or other. So far, nobody-- Well, there are-- there were other stories after I came, but it's not from now.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And so there you are, in the-- in the late 1930s, and you know there's trouble in Europe. You're managing the family business. You're running around to all the towns, doing business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, my father was managing [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But did anything change? And what changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were-- there changed a lot that year. Now, for instance, the official-- the leaders of the Polish government started-- see, so long as Pilsudski was marshal-- Jozef Pilsudski-- the Jews didn't feel so much antisemitism, because he was very good for the Jews. He believed in the ethnic groups in Poland. The minute he died, everything changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died in March-- in March or May, 1935. And since he died, a general by the name of Rydz-Smigly took over, and the whole situation changed. Of-- right away, the head of the Polish church, which was a Cardinal Hlond-- this cardinal came out, officially, and he said, we have, in Poland, three and a half a million Jews. Definitely, three million are too many. And from the three million, a half a million we could survive. Jozef Beck, who was the Secretary of State-- the foreign minister-- had a speech. In this speech he said, we have to get rid of three million Jews. We cannot allow to have three million Jews in Poland. Officially they said it. And there was a prime minister-- his name was Skladkowski. And this prime minister-- there were some groups, and they said, we have to beat the Jews. We have to kill the Jews. So he said, we have-- we-- we don't have to kill them. We don't have to beat them. But we have to get rid of them. Economically, first, and then later physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to call this Skladkowski Mr. Owszem. 'Owszem,' in Poland, means 'by all means.' Because he, in the Senate, had a speech, and he said, if you speak about hooliganism, I am against it. But if you speak about depriving them of businesses-- not to let them be doctors and lawyers-- then, he said-- then-- then, he said, 'owszem.' It means 'by all means.' So this-- we felt that all those things started-- started to-- all those things started to change. And with the influence of Germany-- and Poland made a pact. I don't know if you know about it, that in 1939, Poland make a pact with Germany, when Germany took over Czechoslovakia. So the Germans were very shrewd. So they came to Portland, and there was a sliver-- there was a small piece of land. And we called it Zaolzie, because-- this was in southern Poland-- because there was a river, Olza. And behind the Olza was a small piece of land, and once it belonged to Poland. So Germany made a pact with Poland, and they said we-- a nonaggression pact. And they said, we allow you to take Olza. So when Germany took Czechoslovakia, Poland took this piece of land, which we Jews saw that this is a trick, and it was a very big mistake. So Poland was, in this time, together with Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the war, till the September-- before September-- and naturally, in October-- in August, when we find out about the treaty from Ribbentrop and Molotov. And then we knew that the end is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? What did you do, in your family? Did anything change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- no. We didn't do anything. Also, I-- I may-- I must explain that you asked, for the Jews, if the situation changed. The situation changed very much, because in this time the Polish people started to organize in different cooperatives and businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this is '38, '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: '38, '39. And the most-- biggest-- first they started with the villages. And right away, in each village, they had a cooperative of peasants. And they had signs-- [POLISH]. It means, you have to buy-- you buy your own, not buy Jews. [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it say specifically 'Not buy Jews'?

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or it was inferred.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]. 'Don't buy by a Jew.'

speaker: interviewer
text: It did say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It did say. And then they say, [POLISH]. 'You to your own.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that affect your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it affected because all the small businesses had to give up their businesses. But our business started to flourish more-- my father's. Why did it start to flourish? Because they were not equipped yet to have a wholesale outlet for their small stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? They had to come to us, and we doubled or tripled the business. But my father said that this is very temporary. Because till they will be sophisticated enough to have a wholesale outlet, then they will take away the business from us. So, they will take away the business from us. But meanwhile, meanwhile, the small businesspeople from the small villages-- see, the cooperatives-- what they did-- they took away all the produce from the peasants. And they didn't pay them money for the produce. What was-- for the produce, they gave them merchandise. So they-- it was an exchange. So the middleman, the Jew, was pushed out. But they still had to come to us. And my father was smart enough, and he said, this is only temporary. He knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the new-- he knew that, in a year or two, they will be enough sophisticated. They will be enough smart to have a wholesale, and then our business will go down. But it didn't happen until the war started, and the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. When the war started-- the war started September the 1st. The first thing what I did-- I went, and I enlisted. I volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, at this time, 19 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 19 Years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Officially-- officially, they took to the army only people who were 21 years. But I was 19 years. I always was a very strong Polish patriot. I loved Poland. Even I knew Poland don't-- don't like me. But I figured we are born in this country. We live in this country. Like when there was a holiday, a national holiday, all the children-- in school-- all the children walked with flags. I always wanted to walk with a flag. Because this is my country. And I-- even my father was against it, I went. Nobody knew about it. I went, and I enlisted, and I said, I want to join the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the first day of the war. Naturally, they didn't accept me, because they said, we don't have enough uniforms for our regular army. How will we have uniforms for you? I never told that to my parents. But right away, right away, parts of the army started to come through our town to come back, because we were not far from the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they started to come back. And also, thousands and thousands of Jews from the border towns came through our town to run away to the east. So you could see, the army came back, but the civil people went away-- and mostly Jews. In our town, where it was 4,000 people, in the day of-- when the Germans entered our town, in our town there were 25,000 Jews from different towns. Everybody went through our town. And there was a reason why they went through our town. We didn't have a railroad in our town. And there was a shortcut from a town, Tarnow, to the east, to Rzeszow, through the fields, to go through our town. Because if the Jews ran away, when there was a railroad tracks, the Germans used to bomb the railroad tracks, and they used to shoot on the railroad track. So they picked out-- the people picked out a place where there was no reason for the Germans to destroy those tracks. So they came through our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was very clever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So thousands of Jews, they came-- they stop. In the beginning, we used to give them food. We used to give them shelter. There was no more food. There was no-- because it was used up. And also, when the army went through-- who went-- ran away-- they went into the stores, opened the stores, and they told the peasants to rob, to take out from the stores, because the Germans would come eventually in. And as good Polish patriots, we shouldn't leave anything for the Germans to take away. So what they did-- the peasants came in. They took out everything from the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your store, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: From our store, too. I remember there was a case that my father closed the doors, and came a colonel on a horse. And he opened the door. He went in with the horse, and he told the people, come and take anything you want, because we, as good patriots, cannot leave merchandise for the Germans. Because the Germans will be soon here. And this went on. And every day you could see more army. In the beginning, it was orderly retreat. But then you did-- there was no order. Every soldier for himself. And he was hungry. He came into every store, and he took what he wanted. And the Jews who ran away also came. But we fed them as much as possible. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to join the army again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I travelled-- because there was no army. The army fell apart. The first day, we didn't know, because Poland bragged it's such a strong army. But then, later, everything-- They had a few airplanes. So the leaders took the airplanes the first, second day, and they ran away to Romania. They left the Polish people without leadership!

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll pick up the story about what happened when the Germans occupied your town in just one minute.
